title: Jesus Simulator

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Jesus Simulator as a 'narrative visual novel experience' that retells the Gospel through dialogue, key story moments, multiple viewpoints, and interactive mini-games. The text emphasizes authored historical narrative, spiritual reflection, and witnessing events rather than combat, systems-driven simulation, or open-ended RPG play. Single-player mode is directly supported by store metadata. Although one metadata label mentions 'First-Person Adventure' and generic RPG/simulation tags appear, the detailed gameplay copy is much more specific and strongly supports visual_novel as the core identity.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's historical, educational, and narrative-first qualities reasonably well, but several candidates lean more toward adventure, puzzle, or RPG structures than true visual-novel-style progression.
anchor_summary: Jesus Simulator appears to be a single-player, story-first visual novel: dialogue-led progression, authored historical/religious retelling, multiple viewpoints, and light interactive mini-games rather than combat or systems-heavy play.

title: Moto Rush Reborn

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic descriptions consistently identify a level-based motorcycle racing game centered on clearing fast-paced tracks, weaving through traffic, avoiding obstacles, chasing record times, and completing skill objectives. The core loop is explicitly vehicular racing with vehicle control, speed management, track hazards, unlockable levels, and score/time-performance goals rather than simulation realism. The copy also describes 45 levels, collectible symbols, and chapter unlocks, supporting an arcade-style campaign structure with replayable stage mastery. '3D runner' phrasing adds obstacle-dodging flavor, but the dominant identity remains arcade racing.

overall_note: The set contains two clearly relevant skill-racing comps and two looser neighbors. Best matches emphasize stage-based motorcycle control, obstacle navigation, and repeat-run mastery; weaker fits drift toward combat-driving or simpler lane-dodging arcade play.
anchor_summary: Moto Rush Reborn is a single-player, level-based arcade motorcycle game built around high-speed obstacle avoidance, lane-splitting through traffic, jumps/slides/ducking, time-performance goals, and replaying stages for mastery and collectibles. Its identity sits between arcade racing and 3D runner-style reflex challenge rather than simulation or combat racing.
gap_notes: The rail is missing a closer benchmark in the motorcycle endless-runner/obstacle-course lane rather than broader car racers.

There is no candidate that strongly matches the anchor's specific blend of traffic-weaving, stunt inputs, and stage objective replay without adding major extra systems like combat or unusual vehicle abilities.
Several candidates share speed and hazards, but fewer match the anchor's level-structured score/time mastery loop closely.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Closest gameplay fit: motorcycle-focused, level-structured, precision obstacle navigation, stunt/physics inputs, and repeated runs to improve performance. It differs in side-on physics format and broader multiplayer emphasis, but the mastery loop is very aligned.

Very strong on high-speed survival racing, obstacle-dense tracks, jumping, boosting, and finishing stages quickly rather than lap racing. Car/flying mechanics and a more abstract traversal model make it less direct than Trials.

Good adjacent comp for reflex-heavy neon driving, obstacle avoidance, and runner-like stage design. However, it is lighter and more abstract, with less motorcycle-specific handling and less race-structured progression depth.

Shares motorcycle speed and hazardous roads, but its core identity is vehicular combat with weapons, gang progression, and roguelite-style runs. That makes it a noticeably different audience expectation from the anchor's obstacle-mastery racing.

title: RUBATO

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official text is sufficient and consistent. Steam explicitly calls RUBATO an 'experimental, 2D physics-based take on the collect-a-thon genre' and says players move through 'open metroidvania-like areas' across '5 compact, densely packed areas' while uncovering 'secrets and shortcuts in every corner of the world.' That strongly supports a side-scrolling exploration-platformer structure with semi-open interconnected spaces and discovery gating, which fits metroidvania better than the 3d_collectathon archetype because the game is explicitly 2D. The description also mentions interacting with enemies and objects ('use your tongue to lick enemies and objects') and collecting Planet Bits to fix the solar system, supporting moderate action layered onto exploration. Narrative framing is authored and character-focused, but gameplay identity is primarily side-scrolling exploratory platforming.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's exploratory platforming and offbeat collectathon/metroidvania blend reasonably well. Yoku's Island Express is the standout fit because it shares the unusual physics-forward 2D exploration identity. The rest are viable but looser due to either stronger combat/action-metroidvania emphasis, broader adventure structure, or a perspective mismatch.
anchor_summary: RUBATO appears to be a single-player 2D exploratory platformer with collectathon goals, physics-driven interactions, compact semi-open areas, secret hunting, and some metroidvania-style ability/path progression. The closest matches should emphasize side-scrolling exploration, traversal/collection as the core loop, and quirky action-adventure structure over pure combat or purely 3D collectathon play.
gap_notes: No candidate is a truly near-identical benchmark for a 2D physics-based collectathon-metroidvania hybrid, so the rail lacks a clear 'same' comp.
The set leans partly toward general whimsical exploration games; some candidates miss the anchor's collectible-driven structure or 2D-specific traversal identity.
Cavern of Dreams captures dense collectathon exploration well, but its 3D camera and N64-style platformer structure make it notably less precise than the 2D candidates.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Best fit here: 2D exploration-platforming with a physics-centric movement model, collectible/ability progression, and interconnected non-linear world structure.

Shares side-scrolling exploration, quirky tone, questing, item collection, puzzles, and a large weird world, but reads more as broad action-adventure/platformer than a tight collectathon-metroidvania match.
Very solid on 2D interconnected exploration and ability-gated progression, but Ori is more action-platformer/metroidvania and less physics-driven, comedic, or collectathon-centered than RUBATO.
Strong overlap in dense secret hunting, collectible progression, compact worlds, and ability unlocks, but the 3D perspective and classic 3D collectathon structure make it a looser gameplay match.

title: Inky Blinky Bob

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently describe a single-player action-horror campaign built around piloting a weaponized hot air balloon across a chain of islands, scavenging resources, repairing and upgrading the vehicle, managing limited med kits, and fighting failed experiments with mounted weapons plus close-range axe/shotgun combat. The game emphasizes survival pressure, hostile horror theming, freely flying between islands, and objective/gig-based progression toward powering towers and reaching a sealed facility. This strongly supports action_horror as the best fit, with survival-adventure and open-world action-adventure traits as secondary influences. Perspective and exact camera are not stated, so those fields are left empty.

overall_note: The rail has a strong center around survival games with a mobile upgradable vehicle/base, which matches the anchor's most distinctive loop. The best fits emphasize vehicle-first traversal plus scavenging, repairs, upgrades, and combat. Weaker entries drift toward lighter combatless vehicle adventure or more generic survival crafting without the anchor's action-horror pressure.
anchor_summary: Single-player action-horror survival adventure built around piloting and maintaining a weaponized hot air balloon across interconnected islands. Core loop mixes freeform vehicle traversal, scavenging and crafting repairs/upgrades, mounted and on-foot combat, resource pressure, and objective-driven progression toward major facilities.
gap_notes: Best matches are survival/action games where the vehicle is the core progression and survival platform, not just transport.
The set underrepresents the anchor's action-horror identity and mounted/on-foot combat blend; several candidates are more crafting-sandbox than horror-action.

title: Ghost Master: Resurrection

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a single-player horror-themed strategy/adventure game centered on commanding specters, solving puzzles, and resource management across distinct locations. However, the available text does not cleanly support any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. The gameplay reads closest to a god-game / tactical puzzle / strategy hybrid, which is outside the permitted archetype list. While there are strong signals for horror setting, tactical optimization, and unit control, key identity facets needed to force-fit an allowed archetype are missing or contradictory: no reliable perspective, no clear RTS base-building/economy loop, no turn-based tactics framing, no shooter/action framing, and combat itself appears indirect rather than a dominant conventional combat loop. Given the apparent taxonomy gap and lack of a strong allowable match, rejection is the safest outcome.

This strongly supports a classic side-scrolling stage-based beat_em_up identity with dominant melee combat and combo-driven crowd encounters.

overall_note: This is a very strong candidate set for a modern arcade-style beat 'em up. Best matches should be other belt-scrolling brawlers with similarly arcade-structured combat and combo-focused hand-to-hand play.

title: Crimson Desert

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Crimson Desert as a third-person open-world action-adventure/RPG set in a medieval fantasy world, with free exploration across wilderness, cities, ruins, and the Abyss. Core play includes questing, battles, challenging enemies and bosses, multiple weapons and skills, elemental enhancements, dodges/counters/grapples, and traversal via climbing, gliding, and horseback. Progression includes character growth, skill development, equipment, and rebuilding/upgrading the Greymane camp, supporting an action-RPG identity over pure action-adventure. The strongest fit is open_world_fantasy_action_rpg, with secondary overlap into open_world_action_adventure and some soulslike signals from punishing boss-focused combat.

overall_note: The set has several credible open-world fantasy action RPG neighbors. Black Desert Online shares combat feel and side activities, but its MMORPG structure makes it a much weaker rail fit.

The text explicitly says mining occurs 'without enemies or time limits,' which weakens any combat-driven archetype despite the 'hack-and-slash style equipment system' phrasing. Accepting would require forcing the game into a combat, survival, sandbox, or management identity not strongly supported by the evidence.

title: Thomas & Friends: Wonders of Sodor

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store and aggregator copy consistently describes a train-driving simulation built around taking the controls of Thomas and other engines from an interactive first-person cab view. The game emphasizes carrying passengers, hauling freight, shunting, running timetable services, and a free-roam mode, with stories and exploration across Sodor. It is explicitly described as being powered by Train Sim World with realism/simulation technology but accessible controls, strongly supporting transport_sim over racing or adventure archetypes. Single-player only, non-combat, vehicle-control-centric, and simulation-driven structure are all directly supported by the provided text.

overall_note: The proposed rail is very tight. Both candidates match the anchor's underlying gameplay foundation almost exactly: first-person cab operation, route/service structure, simulation-focused train handling, and similar mode profiles. The main difference is tone and accessibility, not core loop.
anchor_summary: A single-player first-person train-driving simulation built around operating rail services from the cab, with story scenarios, timetable runs, shunting, free roam, and accessible-but-simulation-oriented controls. Its clearest gameplay identity is a family-friendly Train Sim World-style transport sim rather than racing or general adventure.
gap_notes: The strongest similarity signal is that the anchor is explicitly powered by Train Sim World, making mainline Train Sim World entries highly expected recommendations.
These comps are mechanically excellent, but they skew more hardcore/real-world simulation than the anchor's family-oriented Thomas presentation.
A fuller rail would likely prioritize Train Sim World entries and other close rail-operation sims before branching into broader vehicle sims.

title: Maid of Sker VR

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic text consistently and concretely describe a first-person survival horror campaign set in Sker Hotel where the player must stay hidden, manage breathing and noise, evade enemies that hunt by sound, solve puzzle mechanisms, explore for clues and notes, and use a limited defensive device only to stun enemies and escape. The copy repeatedly names the game 'survival horror' and emphasizes stealth, close-quarters tension, exploration, and multiple endings shaped by clues, which strongly supports survival_horror as the core gameplay identity. Stealth-action and psychological horror elements are present, but the dominant loop is survival-focused horror exploration and evasion rather than action combat.

overall_note: The rail has one essential inclusion and a couple of reasonable VR horror neighbors, but it drifts toward broader immersive horror rather than tightly matching Maid of Sker VR's stealth-survival loop. One candidate is notably misleading because it is a combat-heavy DLC rather than the requested base game.
anchor_summary: VR-only first-person survival horror centered on stealthy evasion, noise/breath management, environmental interaction, puzzle-solving, clue-driven exploration, and limited nonlethal defense inside a hostile enclosed setting.
gap_notes: The non-VR Maid of Sker is the clear must-keep benchmark and should anchor the rail.

Best fits should emphasize first-person survival horror with evasion, limited defense, puzzle/exploration, and sustained vulnerability; some candidates lean more toward investigation or combat-survival.
Resident Evil 7: Biohazard - End of Zoe is not the base game and its hand-to-hand action focus is a poor gameplay match for this anchor.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Direct flat-screen counterpart with the same core loop: first-person stealth survival horror, sound-sensitive enemies, limited defensive device, clue-driven exploration, and multiple endings.
Story-driven VR horror with first-person exploration, vulnerability, and environmental interaction, but it is more psychological narrative horror than stealth-survival systems horror.

Close-quarters VR horror with investigation and object interaction fits the immersive vulnerability side, though its episodic exorcism/investigation structure is looser than Maid of Sker VR's stealth-evasion survival loop.
It shares VR survival pressure, manual interaction, and some stealth, but scavenging, crafting, resource runs, and much heavier combat make it a noticeably different gameplay identity.

title: Royal Revolt Survivors

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and corpus text consistently describe a top-down Survivor-style action roguelite built around 1-4 player co-op runs, fighting hordes of enemies, stacking upgrades each run, combining weapons and trinkets, and culminating in boss battles. The core loop is much closer to a co-op action roguelite than beat-em-up or RPG alternatives. Strong signals include explicit 'top-down roguelite action,' 'Survivor-style combat,' high replayability, solo and up-to-4-player co-op, unlockable upgrades that grow stronger every run, and diverse enemies plus epic boss battles. Fantasy kingdom/warrior framing supports high_fantasy and heroic tone.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's action-roguelite and horde-survival identity well, but no candidate fully matches the combination of Survivor-style auto/bullet-heaven loop and 1-4 player co-op. The best fits split between loop similarity and mode-profile similarity.
anchor_summary: Royal Revolt Survivors is a top-down Survivor-style action roguelite centered on horde clearing, per-run build stacking, hero differentiation, boss encounters, and a notable 1-4 player co-op mode.
gap_notes: The strongest matches on Survivor-style horde loop are mostly single-player, so the rail underrepresents the anchor's co-op identity.
The strongest matches on 1-4 player top-down co-op action roguelite lean more room/objective-driven than pure Survivor-style arena survival.
Overall selection is relevant, but there is no near-perfect 'same' candidate that combines co-op, top-down action roguelite structure, and explicit Survivor-like swarm progression.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on horde-survival action roguelite loop, boss focus, build synergies, and multiple characters, but weaker mode fit since it is primarily single-player rather than 1-4 co-op.

Strong co-op/top-down/action-roguelite overlap with build synergies and bosses, but it plays more like a room-based hack-and-slash roguelite than a Survivor-style swarm survival game.
Good match on horde survival, hero builds, and boss encounters, but the single-player focus and more Diablo-like retro action framing make it a looser fit than the others.

title: Grind Survivors

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions are consistent and strong: this is a single-player survivors-like/top-down shoot-'em-up action roguelike built around repeated runs, endless enemy waves, bullet-hell dodging, procedurally generated weapons, stacking upgrades, and build-driven survival. However, the current Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a clean fit for a single-player survivors-like/top-down action roguelite shooter. Available shooter and roguelite archetypes are materially mismatched (for example, hero shooter, military FPS, tactical shooter, extraction shooter, loot shooter, or co-op action roguelite). Because the gameplay identity is clear but does not map reliably to an allowed archetype, this title should be rejected rather than force-fit.

overall_note: This candidate set is highly aligned with the anchor’s gameplay identity. The best fits are other survivors-like horde-action roguelites with run-based progression, heavy buildcraft, and arena survival loops; the only slight looseness comes from candidates that lean more manual twin-stick shooting or inter-wave shop structure.
anchor_summary: Grind Survivors is a single-player survivors-like/top-down action roguelite built around repeated runs, dense horde pressure, bullet-hell dodging, rapid upgrade stacking, procedural weapon loot, and endless scaling survival.
gap_notes: The strongest matches are all survivors-like horde survival roguelites, which is the anchor’s clearest gameplay lane despite taxonomy gaps.
Candidates with stronger manual aiming or wave/shop segmentation are still relevant, but a bit less exact than freeform survivors-like snowball runs.
No obvious bad candidate is present; the set mainly differs in degree of structural closeness.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Extremely close match on survivors-like arena loop, horde clearing, bullet-heavy dodging, deep build synergies, weapon crafting, unlock-based progression, and endless power scaling.
Genre-defining survivors-like comp with the same run-based survival structure, escalating swarms, upgrade drafting, and snowball progression, even if its presentation is more minimalist.

Good overlap in short-run survival roguelite structure, dense enemy pressure, dodging, and weapon-centered builds, but it leans more manual twin-stick shooting than typical auto-attack survivors-like play.
